prompt: If photosynthesis had evolved to release chlorine gas instead of oxygen, how would Earth's biosphere plausibly differ today? Think it through; don't just list bullet points.
response:
The first difference is that the end-product of photosynthesis would be chloride anions (Cl⁻), not water.  
Water-splitting in our timeline releases four protons plus molecular oxygen; a chlorine-releasing system must yield two chloride ions instead of ½ O₂.  
That sounds like a bookkeeping change, but its consequences ramify backward through the entire apparatus of life.

1.  Geochemistry becomes oxidative-chloridic.  
   Almost as soon as the new photosystem appears, the atmosphere starts building up Cl₂ at a few parts per million.  Chlorine is only slightly soluble in water, but it disproportionates quickly in the presence of liquid water into HOCl and HCl.  Rivers, rain drops and oceanic surface films therefore stabilize at pH ≈ 1–2.  Limestone begins to redissolve; clay minerals break down; magnesium and calcium wash to the sea as chlorides.  Salinity climbs until the rate of chlorine escape to space equals the rate of new photosynthetic production—this occurs tens- to hundreds-of-fold above modern seawater salinity.  Cells must therefore evolve intracellular salt pumps far more aggressive than anything found in modern halophiles.

2.  Carbon chemistry shifts to poly-chloro skeletons and volatile organics.  
   Chlorine is a powerful electrophile.  At even modest partial pressures it attacks unsaturated hydrocarbons.  Early attempts at classic lipid bilayers—poly-unsaturated chains—would be literally cut in half.  Lipid membranes are replaced by fluorine-rich or perchlorinated polymers (for which chlorination stabilizes C–Cl bonds) or by salt-gelled polypeptides tolerant of very low pH.  Metabolic maps drop glycolysis in favor of perchlorate respiration; the Calvin-Benson cycle becomes a minor detour because Rubisco is quickly inactivated by HOCl.  New carbon-fixation enzymes evolve that use activated two-carbon fragments (dichloroacetate, trichloroacetyl-CoA) instead of 3-phosphoglycerate.

3.  Atmospheric composition precludes aerobic eukaryotes.  
   There is never an “oxygen holocaust”; instead the planet experiences a “chlorine holocaust” where the dominant atmospheric oxidizer is Cl––the overwhelming oxidation potential stunts the evolution of mitochondria.  Respiration in the alternate biosphere is chlorate- or perchlorate-based rather than oxygen-based.  ATP yields per electron are much lower; organisms remain obligately microbial, although individual cells may form macroscopic sheets or extracellular polymer ‘slime reefs’ many meters thick.  Endosymbiosis does occur, but it is between chemo-lithotrophs that synthesize chlorite and green sulfur bacteria able to oxidize ferrous surface clays; the resulting organelles become the energy centers of amoeboid
